title: Westward expansion: social and cultural development
description: Test your skills on the social and cultural effects of westward expansion on the West!
topic_path: Westward expansion: social and cultural development | 6.3
Westward expansion: social and cultural development

assessment_items.question_content:
> “I am looking rather seedy now while holding down my claim
> And my [[☃ definition 1]] are not always served the best,
> And the mice play shyly 'round me as I nestle down to rest
> In my little old sod shanty in the West.

> “Yet I rather like the novelty of living in this way
> Though my bill of fare is always rather tame,
> But I'm happy as a clam on the land of Uncle Sam
> In my little old sod shanty on my claim. . . .

> “My clothes are plastered o'er with dough, I'm looking like a fright
> And everything is scattered 'round the room,
> But I wouldn't give the freedom that I have out in the West
> For the table of the Eastern man's old home.

>-Source: Oliver Edwin Murray, an excerpt from the song “The Little Old Sod Shanty On My Claim,” 1870s

**The song lyrics most directly reflected which of the following developments during the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: mass migration to rural areas in the Midwest
construction of the transcontinental railroad
discovery of gold and silver in California and Nevada
creation of cooperative organizations for farmers

assessment_items.question_content:
> “I am looking rather seedy now while holding down my claim
> And my [[☃ definition 1]] are not always served the best,
> And the mice play shyly 'round me as I nestle down to rest
> In my little old sod shanty in the West.

> “Yet I rather like the novelty of living in this way
> Though my bill of fare is always rather tame,
> But I'm happy as a clam on the land of Uncle Sam
> In my little old sod shanty on my claim. . . .

> “My clothes are plastered o'er with dough, I'm looking like a fright
> And everything is scattered 'round the room,
> But I wouldn't give the freedom that I have out in the West
> For the table of the Eastern man's old home.

>-Source: Oliver Edwin Murray, an excerpt from the song “The Little Old Sod Shanty On My Claim,” 1870s

**The point of view expressed in the quotation above is most likely that of:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a federal homestead recipient.
a transcontinental railroad owner.
a person searching for religious freedom.
a formerly enslaved person.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “I am looking rather seedy now while holding down my claim
> And my [[☃ definition 1]] are not always served the best,
> And the mice play shyly 'round me as I nestle down to rest
> In my little old sod shanty in the West.

> “Yet I rather like the novelty of living in this way
> Though my bill of fare is always rather tame,
> But I'm happy as a clam on the land of Uncle Sam
> In my little old sod shanty on my claim. . . .

> “My clothes are plastered o'er with dough, I'm looking like a fright
> And everything is scattered 'round the room,
> But I wouldn't give the freedom that I have out in the West
> For the table of the Eastern man's old home.

>-Source: Oliver Edwin Murray, an excerpt from the song “The Little Old Sod Shanty On My Claim,” 1870s

**The song lyrics suggest that people migrating west during the 1870s most typically sought to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: achieve self-sufficiency and independence.
gain religious freedom and flee intolerance.
start families with women living in the area.
find work for major trading companies.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Whose voice was first sounded on this land? The voice of the red people, who had but bows and arrows. . . . What has been done in my country I did not want, did not ask for it; white people going through my country. . . . The white children have surrounded me and have left me nothing but an island. When we first had this land we were strong, now are melting like snow on the hillside, while you are grown like spring grass. . . . When the white man comes in my country he leaves a trail of blood behind him.” 

> -Source: Red Cloud (Mahipua Luta) of the Oglala Sioux, Report of the Board of Indian Commissioners, 1871

**Which of the following developments contributed most directly to the conditions described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of government policies that enabled western migration by white settlers
the introduction of federal laws that required indigenous children to attend assimilation schools
the initiation of treaties between the United States and indigenous nations to protect indigenous land
the environmental regulation of the American West to protect the endangered species of American buffalo

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Whose voice was first sounded on this land? The voice of the red people, who had but bows and arrows. . . . What has been done in my country I did not want, did not ask for it; white people going through my country. . . . The white children have surrounded me and have left me nothing but an island. When we first had this land we were strong, now are melting like snow on the hillside, while you are grown like spring grass. . . . When the white man comes in my country he leaves a trail of blood behind him.” 

> -Source: Red Cloud (Mahipua Luta) of the Oglala Sioux, Report of the Board of Indian Commissioners, 1871

**The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: violent conflicts between indigenous communities and white settlers over land and resources
disagreements between the two major political parties over whether the United States should expand westward
debates over the extension of citizenship rights and protections to indigenous peoples
disputes over federal assimilation policies and their impact on the dwindling indigenous nations

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Whose voice was first sounded on this land? The voice of the red people, who had but bows and arrows. . . . What has been done in my country I did not want, did not ask for it; white people going through my country. . . . The white children have surrounded me and have left me nothing but an island. When we first had this land we were strong, now are melting like snow on the hillside, while you are grown like spring grass. . . . When the white man comes in my country he leaves a trail of blood behind him.” 

> -Source: Red Cloud (Mahipua Luta) of the Oglala Sioux, Report of the Board of Indian Commissioners, 1871

**People who shared the author’s ideas at the time most typically sought to achieve their goals by doing which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: resisting migration and fighting American forces
petitioning the government for American citizenship
migrating north to Canada for more plots of land
filing legal challenges in federal courts against white settlers

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Hereafter no Indian nation or tribe within the territory of the United States shall be acknowledged or recognized as an independent nation, tribe, or power with  whom the United States may contract by treaty. Provided, further, that nothing herein contained shall be construed to invalidate or impair the obligation of any treaty heretofore lawfully made and ratified with any such Indian nation or tribe.” 

> -Source: excerpt from the Indian Appropriation Act, 1871

**Which of the following most likely motivated Congress to pass the law in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a federal effort to remove indigenous nations from land and claim it for white settlers
a divisive court ruling that excluded indigenous land from state laws and guaranteed an indigenous right to sovereignty
an armed invasion by several indigenous nations aiming to reclaim land in the northeastern states
a treaty between France and indigenous nations that created an alliance to conquer western states

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Hereafter no Indian nation or tribe within the territory of the United States shall be acknowledged or recognized as an independent nation, tribe, or power with  whom the United States may contract by treaty. Provided, further, that nothing herein contained shall be construed to invalidate or impair the obligation of any treaty heretofore lawfully made and ratified with any such Indian nation or tribe.” 

> -Source: excerpt from the Indian Appropriation Act, 1871

**Which of the following was the most immediate result of the law excerpted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the removal of indigenous peoples from desirable lands and relocation to reservations
the rise of the Ghost Dance in indigenous nations across the West to protest federal actions
the start of several armed conflicts between indigenous nations and white settlers
the creation of the Bureau of Indian Affairs to protect indigenous peoples’ rights and interests

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Hereafter no Indian nation or tribe within the territory of the United States shall be acknowledged or recognized as an independent nation, tribe, or power with  whom the United States may contract by treaty. Provided, further, that nothing herein contained shall be construed to invalidate or impair the obligation of any treaty heretofore lawfully made and ratified with any such Indian nation or tribe.” 

> -Source: excerpt from the Indian Appropriation Act, 1871

**This law undermined which of the following federal actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Supreme Court decision which guaranteed indigenous sovereignty
the federal law that created the reservation system in the American West
the Fifteenth Amendment that guaranteed all citizens the right to vote
the legislative measure that banned white settlers from taking native land

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the end, the Ghost Dance offered believers, not an immediate and violent rejection of American governance, but an intense spiritual and emotional experience that facilitated their accommodation to American dominance in many areas of Indian life while simultaneously allowing them to seek out health and prosperity on Indian terms. The Ghost Dance, in other words, helped many believers accept conquest while strengthening their resolve to resist assimilation.”

>-Source: Louis S. Warren, *God’s Red Son: The Ghost Dance Religion and the Making of Modern America,* 2017

**The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: indigenous people’s efforts to preserve their culture
indigenous acceptance of native boarding schools
federal efforts to ban native religious practices
federal attempts to ban native languages in schools

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the end, the Ghost Dance offered believers, not an immediate and violent rejection of American governance, but an intense spiritual and emotional experience that facilitated their accommodation to American dominance in many areas of Indian life while simultaneously allowing them to seek out health and prosperity on Indian terms. The Ghost Dance, in other words, helped many believers accept conquest while strengthening their resolve to resist assimilation.”

>-Source: Louis S. Warren, *God’s Red Son: The Ghost Dance Religion and the Making of Modern America,* 2017

**Which of the following contributed most directly to the trend described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of government policies forcing indigenous communities to relocate to reservations
the establishment of federal agencies aimed at representing indigenous interests and protecting their communities from federal intervention
the formation of an alliance between indigenous nations to resist American efforts to expand onto native lands
the legislation protecting indigenous lands under federal law from invasion by foreign countries

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the end, the Ghost Dance offered believers, not an immediate and violent rejection of American governance, but an intense spiritual and emotional experience that facilitated their accommodation to American dominance in many areas of Indian life while simultaneously allowing them to seek out health and prosperity on Indian terms. The Ghost Dance, in other words, helped many believers accept conquest while strengthening their resolve to resist assimilation.”

>-Source: Louis S. Warren, *God’s Red Son: The Ghost Dance Religion and the Making of Modern America,* 2017

**Which of the following most likely contributed to the rise of the “Ghost Dance” referred to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: efforts by the US government to control the indigenous communities in the West
legislative measures that granted citizenship to all indigenous people
declarations of war against indigenous nations by the federal government
decline in indigenous populations following several infectious diseases
